posts.name: Andrew Hamilton
posts.cooked:
Apparently they’ll be using DIAM for their 2014’s, from Bourgogne Blanc to Montrachet. Given their premox track record I can’t help but think this is a good move.

http://www.wineanorak.com/wineblog/wine-science/domaine-leflaives-switch-to-diam

link_counts.title: Domaine Leflaive’s switch to DIAM – Jamie Goode's wine blog

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Excellent news. Let’s hope it makes a difference.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Yep, this can only potentially ‘help’ in the battle against pre-mox.

The article certainly was interesting, and noted a few others that have switched to screw caps on most of their whites - but still not all because of concerns about ‘perception’. So my question - what’s worse . . . perception or pre-mox?

Cheers!

posts.name: Frank Drew
posts.cooked: I thought the principal argument for DIAM was TCA prevention; how will they help prevent premature oxidation? Have we settled on natural corks providing an inconsistent seal as the explanation for this malady (and DIAM engineered corks being better seals)?

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Frank Drew:

I thought the principal argument for DIAM was TCA prevention; how will they help prevent premature oxidation? Have we settled on natural corks providing an inconsistent seal as the explanation for this malady (and DIAM engineered corks being better seals)?

Frank,

According to their literature, DIAM’s are more ‘consistent’ with trans ox than cork is because of how they are made. In fact, they offer different densities that they guarantee for different periods of time.

All of that said, these still are made of cork particles, which will all be ‘variable’ in cell sizes, etc. I still have not seen enough ‘data’ to convince me that these will be consistent in oxidation over time as, say, screwcaps will . . .

And no, I don’t think folks have zeroed in as natural corks as being the only or even best explanation for the malady as of now, but others who know more will hopefully pipe in.

Cheers

posts.name: Cameron Hughes
posts.cooked:
Andrew Hamilton:

Apparently they’ll be using DIAM for their 2014’s, from Bourgogne Blanc to Montrachet. Given their premox track record I can’t help but think this is a good move.

Domaine Leflaive’s switch to DIAM – Jamie Goode's wine blog

It doesn’t make sense to me that pre-mox issues in Burgundy are cork-related other than the one-off bad cork…if there was a decline in cork quality it would be seen across many regions…cork quality did not suddenly decline over the past 20 years…Burgundians know and can afford good cork…its not rocket science. Nor does it make sense that “bottling conditions” would be the culprit - bottling line technology and quality has only gone up over the past 25 years and Burgundy can afford good bottling equipment. Neither of these remotely passes the sniff test.

Its far more likely that the issue is production related…Antonio Galloni recently brought up (I can’t recall where) the potential villain as producers having moved away from “browning out” the juice and in effect oxidizing and therefore inoculating the wine against further oxidation effects post-fermentation. I don’t recall exactly what he said, but, I believe his point was there was a move towards “stabilizing” white juice with sulfur to avoid “browning juice” and Galloni’s comment (and largely presented as a theory, not fact) was that, from what he has learned, producers who browned out haven’t seen the premox issues whereas producers who “stabilized” had.

Hi Cameron. If you look at the relative ‘position’ (ie on a graph - I published one, two or three years ago) of the combination of acidity and potential alcohol, it’s clear that fermented chardonnay from Burgundy is largely not the same as it was 25 years ago. And that’s before anyone decides to brown out or whatever. Climate is responsible for that.

From the research that I’ve seen and discussed with the technical director of the BIVB, plus talking with a few dozen winemakers each year, for years, about the problem (those that admit to a problem!) it seems clear that the base wine is not quite what it once was, and it has a higher inherent potential to oxidise than was once the case.

Now all corks are different - it’s a broad distribution of oxygen permeabilites - the worst oxidise faster than the best - there’s no magic about that. The best will still do 20 years, but maybe there are only a couple of those in your case - we know what happens to the rest, it’s only a question of time. Corks always had a broad distribution, though anecdotally they are less good than before, but who can really say(?) Whilst it is (to me) a false assertion that corks alone are to blame, at least people can pick a consistent oxygen permeability in line with the best (or more?) corks - so you will have maybe all good at 20 years. In a way it’s a sticking-plaster solution, but at least for 10 years (with ‘only’ DIAM5) it seems an efficient sticking-plaster.

Of-course none of us have ever had the chance to taste a wine after 20 years sealed by DIAM - I hope there won’t be a different type of disappointment in wait…

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Cameron Hughes:

It doesn’t make sense to me that pre-mox issues in Burgundy are cork-related other than the one-off bad cork…if there was a decline in cork quality it would be seen across many regions…cork quality did not suddenly decline over the past 20 years…Burgundians know and can afford good cork…its not rocket science. Nor does it make sense that “bottling conditions” would be the culprit - bottling line technology and quality has only gone up over the past 25 years and Burgundy can afford good bottling equipment. Neither of these remotely passes the sniff test.

Cameron- I don’t think anyone claims it’s exclusively corks. But since the premox problem frequently occurs with some but not all bottles in the same case, it’s hard not to think that less-than-perfect-seal corks are one of the necessary conditions for premox.

I think everyone suspects that changes in winemaking are the other half of the explanation. If as Bill says, white Burgundy chemically different than it was 25 years ago, the wines now may simply be more vulnerable to lower levels of air penetration.

posts.name: Cameron Hughes
posts.cooked:
billnanson:

Hi Cameron. If you look at the relative ‘position’ (ie on a graph - I published one, two or three years ago) of the combination of acidity and potential alcohol, it’s clear that fermented chardonnay from Burgundy is largely not the same as it was 25 years ago. And that’s before anyone decides to brown out or whatever. Climate is responsible for that.

From the research that I’ve seen and discussed with the technical director of the BIVB, plus talking with a few dozen winemakers each year, for years, about the problem (those that admit to a problem!) it seems clear that the base wine is not quite what it once was, and it has a higher inherent potential to oxidise than was once the case.

Now all corks are different - it’s a broad distribution of oxygen permeabilites - the worst oxidise faster than the best - there’s no magic about that. The best will still do 20 years, but maybe there are only a couple of those in your case - we know what happens to the rest, it’s only a question of time. Corks always had a broad distribution, though anecdotally they are less good than before, but who can really say(?) Whilst it is (to me) a false assertion that corks alone are to blame, at least people can pick a consistent oxygen permeability in line with the best (or more?) corks - so you will have maybe all good at 20 years. In a way it’s a sticking-plaster solution, but at least for 10 years (with ‘only’ DIAM5) it seems an efficient sticking-plaster.

Of-course none of us have ever had the chance to taste a wine after 20 years sealed by DIAM - I hope there won’t be a different type of disappointment in wait…

Hey Bill - thanks for the thoughtful note…I’m curious about the climate part…do we have degree days chart for the region? Have temps really gone up to drive higher alcs? Can we draw a straight line to climate for that? I should add this isn’t about questioning climate change; instead, I wonder if designer yeasts or simply higher brix conversion due to cleanliness or technology could be playing a role here?

RE: Diam. I use the corks myself and have for about 8 years now. I highly recommend them and we use them on our Chardonnay’s. I have used other agglo’s products for even longer and, when they are not ruined by the insidious low-level TCA that forced us to switch to DIAM, they perform very well over time. I believe that the better tech of the DIAMs will perform very well for Burgundies producers.

Having said that, I think we both agree corks had little to do with Burgundies premox issue.

Cheers!

Cameron

I agree with the highlighted portion. Also I think a huge culprit is bladder/membrane presses vs. basket/vertical presses. You will have less yield per ton with the basket but it helps aerate the juice prior to fermentation. I think much less so2 in the past was used during the juice stage on compared to current norms.

I used DIAM on my whites for 2 vintages and was happy with them for the most part. We may head back that way in the future at some point. I switched my cork supplier and have been very happy with the results from a TCA and overall quality standpoint. We were most worried about consumer perception and we got literally zero feedback either negative or positive. No one seemed to notice.

posts.name: L e o F r o k i c
posts.cooked: Personally I’m in love with DIAM corks. This year we’ve been going thru 2011 white, 1er cru burgs that been sealed with DIAM and there was 0 corked, 0 oxidised and best thing of all was that they were all uniformed in taste, 0 bottle variation which has never happened with regular corks. I do not know how will they taste 10 or 20 year down the road but as of now they’re awesome.
posts.display_username: L e o F r o k i c

posts.name: Andrew Nielsen
posts.cooked:
Within 5 years, no serious producer of white Burgundy will be using cork. Comtes Lafon, De Montille and now Leflaive are using DIAM and Benjamin Leroux is moving to screwcap in all markets other than France and China, which he thinks are not ready to accept screwcap just yet. The potential to use lower SO2 levels at bottling with screwcap vs DIAM is a plus in it’s favour he believes.

While I agree DIAM is not the “solution”, it will definitely decrease the incidence. I personally believe it is a combination of what Bill and Jon have said, the move away from vertical presses, “modern” vinification protocols (including pneumatic pressing, whole bunch, the protection of must with SO2 etc), and climate change.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
larry schaffer:

So my question - what’s worse . . . perception or pre-mox?

It doesn’t matter how well the wines age if you can’t sell them.

John, perhaps that’s true, but the problem first reared its ugly head in a major way with the 1995 and 1996 wines, which were 21 and 20 years ago respectively.

posts.name: Kevin Harvey
posts.cooked: FWIW, I seems to me that Leflaive premox problem began with the change of winemakers around 2006. Many practices were changed including the introduction of a new press. It’s hard to see how a change of corks will rectify that.

posts.cooked:
Cameron Hughes:

Whoops, its was Jon Bonne, not Galloni, that raised the issue of browning out the juice as protection from premox. I suddenly remembered where i saw it…A New Era for White Wine - PUNCH

A lot of folks have been talking about this for years FWIW.

Exactly. Couldn’t agree with you more.

posts.name: Eric Lundblad
posts.cooked:
Kevin Harvey:

FWIW, I seems to me that Leflaive premox problem began with the change of winemakers around 2006. Many practices were changed including the introduction of a new press. It’s hard to see how a change of corks will rectify that.

You made an interesting (to me) comment earlier that, in the prior winemaking regime, Leflaive used a Vaslin press. Which means their performance isn’t a data point for must oxidation vs hard/aggressive pressing (i.e. pressing skin/stem phenols) or both being the/a key to beating premox.

posts.name: Kevin Harvey
posts.cooked:
Eric Lundblad:
Kevin Harvey:

FWIW, I seems to me that Leflaive premox problem began with the change of winemakers around 2006. Many practices were changed including the introduction of a new press. It’s hard to see how a change of corks will rectify that.

You made an interesting (to me) comment earlier that, in the prior winemaking regime, Leflaive used a Vaslin press. Which means their performance isn’t a data point for must oxidation vs hard/aggressive pressing (i.e. pressing skin/stem phenols) or both being the/a key to beating premox.

Eric,
The Vaslin press is both phenolic and oxidative. It exposes the must to a lot of air as it presses so the “browning” is essentially automatic.

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked:
Kevin Harvey:

FWIW, I seems to me that Leflaive premox problem began with the change of winemakers around 2006. Many practices were changed including the introduction of a new press. It’s hard to see how a change of corks will rectify that.

When a domaine known for being one of few relatively unaffected by premox becomes a new poster boy for premoxed wines, wouldn’t it make sense and be fairly easy to RETURN to the previous practices? Or is the current winemaker completely clueless?

Also, do you know what other practices were changed after Pierre Morey left? And what was the last vintage that Pierre made?

William Kelley:
Kevin Harvey:

FWIW, I seems to me that Leflaive premox problem began with the change of winemakers around 2006. Many practices were changed including the introduction of a new press. It’s hard to see how a change of corks will rectify that.

Exactly. Couldn’t agree with you more.

Whilst 2006 was indeed a watershed in lifeless wine from the domaine, and fortunately it’s never been so bad since then, most long-time buyers (with a problem) point to ~2002 as the start of the premox issue chez Leflaive, so actually predating the retirement of Pierre. That doesn’t mean they didn’t change presses in 2002, though…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Eric Remy joined Leflaive in 2003, coming from Génot-Boulanger. I don’t know if there was a gradual transition before Pierre Morey’s official retirement in 2008. I seem to remember hearing that Remy joined as maitre de chai, not sure.

One can debate whether corks are the culprit but facts shows that screwcaps are the saviour!!

I don’t know that screwcaps are the savior, but they provide consistency, and that’s something that one might find useful. The move to Diam is interesting, but questionable. It seems like it’s done because as Larry suggests, there’s a perception issue. So rather than use something that can be made to spec, people would rather use a closure that’s just as “artificial” but seems “natural” because it reminds them of a plug of bark. it’s a shame in a way, that perception matters so much.

posts.name: Dale Bowers
posts.cooked: Don’t want to drift this topic, but why hasn’t someone put their wine under both cork AND screwcap? Granted you would still have a potential TCA issue, but the issue of closure would be addressed and people who claim cork is “romantic” get to have their cake and eat it too.

Dale Bowers:

Don’t want to drift this topic, but why hasn’t someone put their wine under both cork AND screwcap? Granted you would still have a potential TCA issue, but the issue of closure would be addressed and people who claim cork is “romantic” get to have their cake and eat it too.

This has been done a couple of times outside of Burgundy. Not aware of any Burg producers who have done side-by-side comparison bottlings, though.

bet Anne-Claude is spinning in her grave. She wouldn’t even allow UPC bar

I reckon Marchand-Tawse would fit this bill. Pascal has a bottling machine that can handle both screwcap and cork as all of his Burgundy wines under his Marchand & Burch label (a joint venture between Pascal and Jeff Burch, owner of Howard Park wines) are under screwcap. My assumption (and I could probably reach out to Pascal and confirm) is that the Marchand-Tawse bottlings and the Marchand & Burch bottlings are largely identical other than the label and closure.
